sentences.text: Well, perhaps in the best way, it would be $10.
Right.
I forgot to mention, the soup kitchen produced an one-pot meal, which was a little bit thicker soup.
I don't want to-- I don't want that you think that was a-- a dinner served.
It was just a one-pot meal.
And also, you got a slice of bread.
The bread was very restrictive.
I believe it was 100 gram a day per-- per person.
So you try to save part of the bread for the morning, breakfast, or for the evening, because when you ate it-- when you ate it, it was all you had.
And I remember my mother tried to doctor up the meal with a little bit-- whatever she-- she could get otherwise too.
And I remember also that from that time on, I believe for years to come, I was always hungry.
It was my growing years, and I needed perhaps more.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We were not allowed to buy at all.
So what we did?
We bartered.
We bartered with other Jews.
It was an giving-- giving and take.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My free time-- I forgot to tell you too that we worked six days, not like here 40 hours.
We worked perhaps 10 hours a day, 6 days a week.
And we had only the Sunday free.
And the Sundays I always spent in the JUL, in the--
--group of Aron Menczer.
And I met a lot of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Jews in my age group, girls and boys.
And that was actually the happiest times I had, with them for a few hours, because when I had to go home, it was depressing.
And when I had to work, it was-- I was living in a different world.
So, coming back on Sunday, that was the-- the time I looked always forward to.
No, this was after, because I worked-- actually, I was not working in a work camp.
I was sleeping at home.
I had to come only every morning to the work place, and every evening I had to leave.
I was-- right.
'Free' under quotation marks, because I had to go the next day to an German construction firm, which-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which took me as a worker, as a unpaid worker, as a laborer, for nothing.
But I was under the control of the Gestapo, because I had to be every morning there.
They had records.
And I had to work six days a week.
And I was only allowed to go home in the evening to sleep.
And only my Sunday was free.
That was actually the biggest privilege we had.
It went 'til a few days before November 21st, 1941, when I left Austria and escaped to Yugoslavia.
November the 21st, 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that I would like to tell you a little bit more about.

When my cousin Ernst came to me and said, Bob, my family is leaving for Shanghai on the Trans-Siberian railway from Vienna to Harbin and from Harbin to Shanghai, you have to leave, and I have to give you some other news.
Your name came up on the next list of the transport to Poland.
We knew quite a bit.
First of all, we knew that the Viennese Gestapo asked the Jewish community, the [NON-ENGLISH], for lists, and particular lists in 1,000 names.
And he worked in the department which had all those lists.
They were actually compiling the lists for the Gestapo.
No, the Jewish-- Israeli-- [NON-ENGLISH].
The JUL was only part of that.
And Ernst worked in the department of the-- it was called the [NON-ENGLISH].
And the earliest transports from Vienna to Theresienstadt-- which was not Auschwitz or it was not the camps in Poland, but was a central holding camp in Czechoslovakia.
The earliest ones were in October of 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He came to me and he says, Bob, your list-- your name came up in one of the lists.
You have to leave.
And on the same evening, or the next morning, I went to Aron-- I didn't go to work.
I went to Aron Menczer.
And I say to him, this has happened.
I am losing in a few days my quarters where I have my bed, because my family is going with the Trans-Siberian railway to Shanghai.
I have the knowledge to Ernst, my cousin, that my name is in the next list of a transport to Poland.
And Aron Menczer at that time had a way of smuggling out Jews out of his group, young Jews, to-- to Yugoslavia.
About Aron?
I'll tell you.
A lot of the children coming to him lost their fathers in the concentration camps.
By concentration camps, at that time was Dachau and Buchenwald, concentration camps in Germany.
So they were already half orphans.
Of some of them, the mothers disappeared out of certain reasons I really don't know.
And they were full orphans.
And Aron had now the responsibility of feeding them, giving shelter to them at night and at day.
And Aron was looking for ways to get those children out of his hands.
And he found an man, an SR man living in Gratz, which had an restaurant and a pig farm by the name of Schleich.
S-C-H-L-E-I-C-H.
He was actually an Austrian Nazi with the brown shirts, which was called SA.
And he had an arrangement with Aron, or with the JUL, that he would take a Jew-- a Jew for 600 mark and send him with-- under the guidance of smugglers he knew, to Yugoslavia.

And Aron did send children in the age-- in the age from, let's say 15 to 17, which were orphans, which were actually on his-- under his supervision, in groups of 10, in groups of 8, to Gratz, to this guy by the name of Schleich.
And they came to Yugoslavia.
I knew a lot of friends of mine, which I saw and-- and had contact with, just were not there anymore the next day or in the-- in the next week when I came.
They were not here anymore.
And when I asked, you know, where's so-and-so, they said, oh, Aron, sent them on this-and-this transport to Yugoslavia.
So I had the knowledge already that that existed.
Yes.
Not only that, the Jewish-- Aron Menczer had no money.
He was a very sincere fellow.
And everything he owned, he gave actually to the children.
He worked without salary.
And the money he needed to send the children, because Schleich wanted 600 marks per person, Aron got from the Jewish community center, from the Jewish-Israeli [NON-ENGLISH].
And they paid, actually.
They gave him the money so that he could pay Schleich.
It was not Aron Menczer's money.
It was the collective money of the Jewish community which made it possible [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] for those escapes.
We knew that an large camp exist in Theresienstadt.
Don't forget, at that time, mail service existed between the camp of Theresienstadt and the families in Vienna.

We got letters from people which were deported to Theresienstadt asking, for example, about-- about members in the family, and asking also if it would be possible to send them money, or send them food, which we didn't have anyway, but send them parcels.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Every letter was censored, which means it was opened and closed by a paper stripe from-- from the-- from the German authorities.
Yet sometimes, when family members sent money to Theresienstadt, we knew from other letters returning that they got the money.
So we knew about conditions in Theresienstadt.
Dachau and Buchenwald, also people were allowed to write a postcard a month and were allowed to receive a postcard back.
So we knew about certain conditions.
They-- they were not able to tell us in words, 'we were beaten' or 'we were badly treated.' But they knew how to-- to write certain things and we knew how to read between lines.
And we knew also that in Poland different camps existed, because out of certain camps, we never got any answers back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was no mail service to Auschwitz.
No.
We only heard rumors, unsubstantiated rumors.
But at that time, we rather started to believe those rumors.
Because we knew how the Germans acted.
We knew how the people were treated.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people which came back out of the concentration camps, like my cousin from Dachau, he told us certain things.
He didn't tell anything-- he didn't wanted to go in much detail, but we-- we started to get the message.
I took it.
And I also asked him, very, very sincerely, Aron, please help me.
I don't want to go to-- to Poland.
And by the way, I am not alone.
I have my mother.
And, you know, I cannot leave her.
And he did-- he said, OK.
I know your situation.
And he made it possible for me to go to Schleich.
Now, that is not as easy as I tell you, because at that time no Austrian Jew was allowed to go on a railroad, and Vienna and Gratz is quite a distance.
So we had to take an [INAUDIBLE] to go illegally on the rail, but we had to buy tickets.
We had to--
Of course, there were controls on the railway stations.
But we are also able to slip by controls.
And Aron told us certain ways how to do that.
And that was why he was able to send the children to go.
And when-- when we came to-- when we-- when he told us, he told us only about Schleich and his address of the restaurant in Gratz.
Right.
Yes.
My aunt and cousin came with us.
And when we reached Schleich, the restaurant, he told us about the pig farm he had in the vicinity of Gratz.
And he brought us to the pig farm.
But we didn't know anything else--
--down the line.
Yes.
I'm glad that you asked me that.
We took only-- I-- I took only a hand bag, but I put on two shirts, and I had a pullover, and I tried to wear as much as underwear as-- as I could.
And my mother did the same, and, of course, everybody else, because nothing else we could take along with us.
And this guy immediately knew who we were and took us to his pig farm.
He had one of the [INAUDIBLE] small trucks at the time.
And we were already, I believe, five or six people at the time in this little room in the restaurant, because we were not alone.
Others joined us.
No.
And we didn't know about them.
But suddenly, somebody else came into the room, somebody else.
And most of the people which came in the room were children from Aron Menczer.
And I didn't know that Aron Menczer sent them.
And some of them I knew from Vienna.
Some of them were German childrens-- children.
You see, Aron Menczer had an pipeline, also from Berlin, from Gelsenkirchen, from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Frankfurt am Main, from other German communities where Jews still existed.
And they sent their children which had no parents to Vienna, because they somehow knew about this line to Yugoslavia.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was on this pig farm for perhaps two days.
I don't recall any more.
The whole way to Yugoslavia took five or six days.
And, I must say, Schleich brought us food.
It was perhaps better than the-- the soup kitchen from the [NON-ENGLISH].
And then one day, he came and he say, today we go to Spielberg [NON-ENGLISH], which was the border town between Austria, Carinthia, and Yugoslavia at that time.
And I will bring you to an farmer, which is a smuggler.
And this farmer smuggled saccharine and cigarettes for years back-- even before Hitler occupied Austria, he was already a smuggler-- to Yugoslavia.
And he brought back certain things that I don't know.
And he was very familiar about the border.
And the border in Spielberg [NON-ENGLISH], the border I'm talking about, is not the road.
It's the mountains.
It's the woods.
It's for me the wilderness.
And don't forget, I left on 21st of November.
I was in Spielberg [NON-ENGLISH] perhaps on the 23rd of November.
That was November, '41, a very harsh winter, high snow and cold.
And by no means I had shoes for those conditions.
I had regular shoes only and regular clothes.
So when we-- when we reached Spielberg [NON-ENGLISH] and he gave us into the hands of this Austrian farmer, we were bought into a little room.
He had straw on the floor.
And he say, we-- we'll cross the border during the night and we will go in single file.
I will be the leader.
The next one will go after me and after me and after me.
And if you lose the person in front of you, that's it.
And it will be night.
It will be very difficult.
Don't take anything with you.
We were at that time perhaps 12 people, or perhaps 14.
By the way, on the same farm, the same day, I met an-- an-- a boy my age by the name of Manny Issler.
Issler, I-S-S-L-E-R.
And this boy was from the group of Aron Menczer.
He damaged his ankle and he had to stay back.
And why I mention him to you is because he's from Gelsenkirchen, a German.
He was very religious.
He was strictly kosher.
He laid his tefillin every day.
And my mother had 10 mark and I had 10 marks.
And we bartered the 10 marks for eggs with the farmer.
And we had the eggs hard-boiled, because that was the only thing he would eat.
And we left him the eggs.
During the night, around 11:00 or 12:00, the farmer picked us up.
No.
I may have known.
But I don't remember anymore.
The name of Schleich will always stay with me.
The name of Aron Menczer will always stay with me.
But certain names I-- I don't remember.
And we went on a trek-- trek to the mountains, to the snow, to the woods.
And we came, after a few hours of walking under the most strenuous conditions-- and we were not very strong at the time.
And I-- I remember a lot of people threw their baggage, whatever they had, away.
We--
We came to a-- to an area where he say, this is the frontier.
On one side, German guards walking.
And on the other side, the people walking already Yugoslavian soldiers.
We have to wait for a time period when there's nobody here, and then we cross.
In the-- the youngest was perhaps 12, 13 years old.
And the oldest was around my mother, my aunt's age.
I would say, let's say 40.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we crossed, and we walked again for another hour, hour and a half.
And we came to the town of Maribor.
